skill.name: writing-skills
skill.description: Use when creating, editing, or validating AI agent skills with test-driven documentation methods.
skill.summary: Create and validate agent skills using pressure tests, iteration, and clear authoring rules.
skill.author: CodingCossack

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are markdown examples, documentation URLs, or words used in authoring guidance. The real executable surface is render-graphs.js, which runs local Graphviz and writes SVG files under a user-supplied skill directory. No evidence found of credential theft, covert networking, persistence, or malicious prompt injection.

medium_findings.title: Local Graphviz Execution Helper
medium_findings.description: The bundled Node.js helper imports child_process and runs local Graphviz commands. The command strings are fixed and dot input is passed through stdin, so command injection is not evident. It still executes a local binary and should be reviewed before marketplace publication.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The executable code path is real and confirmed. Risk is limited because commands are static and no user-controlled shell command string was found.

medium_findings.title: Writes Generated Files Under User-Supplied Directory
medium_findings.description: The helper resolves a user-provided skill directory, creates a diagrams folder, and writes SVG and DOT outputs there. This is legitimate for a rendering utility, but it can modify files outside the package if the user supplies another path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem writes are directly present and semantically intentional. The impact is moderate because writes are predictable output files and not hidden persistence.

medium_findings.title: Agent Pressure-Test Prompts Need Context
medium_findings.description: The examples include urgency and authority-style prompts for testing skill compliance. They are documented test scenarios rather than hidden instructions to this audit, but marketplace reviewers should understand their purpose.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text intentionally pressures an agent, but it is framed as test data. I found no instruction to bypass security analysis or override marketplace review.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Mostly Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: Many external command findings come from fenced examples, inline command references, Graphviz examples, or documentation snippets. These are not executed by the skill runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review showed the cited lines are documentation examples. They explain authoring practices and do not create execution paths by themselves.

low_findings.title: Documentation Image URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs in the copied best-practices document point to Claude documentation and image CDN assets. No code path fetches these URLs automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation references and image sources. I found no evidence of network calls from executable code.

low_findings.title: Home Directory Paths Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: References to ~/.claude/skills and ~/.codex/skills describe where personal skills live. They are not hidden file reads or writes in executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are examples and workflow text. No automatic access to those hidden directories was found in the executable helper.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and C2 Keyword Alerts Are Semantic False Positives
low_findings.description: Static blocker alerts were triggered by ordinary words, citations, image URL parameters, or documentation content. No encryption routine, C2 endpoint, downloader, or beaconing behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The high-risk labels do not match the reviewed context. I found no code implementing cryptography or command-and-control behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: child_process.execSync Usage
dangerous_patterns.description: render-graphs.js uses execSync for Graphviz rendering and for checking whether dot is installed. This is the main real dangerous pattern in the package.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present in executable JavaScript. The command strings are fixed, which reduces but does not eliminate review risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Synchronous File Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: render-graphs.js creates output directories and writes generated diagram files. The behavior is expected, but it touches the filesystem outside a read-only documentation skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and reachable when the helper runs. They are not stealthy and are limited to diagram outputs.

content.value_statement: Agent skills often fail when they are not tested under realistic pressure. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured method for writing clear skills and verifying behavior before deployment.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to write skill frontmatter and body content for discoverability.
Maps test-driven development concepts to skill creation and editing.
Provides pressure-test methods for checking agent compliance before deployment.
Defines when to use flowcharts, examples, supporting files, and reference documents.
Includes a local Graphviz helper for rendering dot diagrams from skill files.
Documents common rationalizations and anti-patterns in discipline-focused skills.
limitations: It depends on separate background knowledge of test-driven development.
The Graphviz helper requires a local dot installation.
It does not validate marketplace package manifests automatically.
It is focused on skill-writing process, not general prose editing.

use_cases.title: Create a New Agent Skill
use_cases.description: Write a skill with clear triggers, scoped examples, and test scenarios before sharing it.

use_cases.title: Improve an Existing Skill
use_cases.description: Find vague instructions, add missing pressure cases, and close rationalization gaps.

use_cases.title: Verify Skill Behavior
use_cases.description: Use baseline and pressure scenarios to check whether agents follow the documented process.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Skill Outline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the writing-skills guidance to outline a new skill for [task]. Include the trigger description, core principle, sections, and one pressure test.

prompt_templates.title: Review Skill Discoverability
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this skill for discoverability and clarity. Check the description, keywords, examples, and whether Claude would know when to load it.

prompt_templates.title: Design Pressure Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Create three pressure scenarios for this skill. Include baseline behavior, expected failure rationalizations, and success criteria after the skill is applied.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor After Failed Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Given these failed pressure-test transcripts, identify the rationalizations and revise the skill to close each loophole without adding unrelated guidance.

output_examples.input: Create a skill for verification before completion.
output_examples.output: A concise skill outline with a trigger-focused description, a core rule, pressure scenarios, and a checklist for deployment readiness.

output_examples.input: Test whether agents follow a TDD skill under pressure.
output_examples.output: Three realistic scenarios combining time pressure, sunk cost, authority, and explicit choices with pass or fail criteria.

output_examples.input: Improve a skill that agents keep skipping.
output_examples.output: A revised description, a rationalization table, stronger red flags, and targeted re-tests for the observed failures.

best_practices: Run baseline scenarios before writing or editing a discipline-focused skill.
Keep the description focused on when to use the skill, not the full workflow.
Use supporting files only for heavy references or reusable tools.
anti_patterns: Writing a skill from intuition without first observing agent failure.
Putting long process summaries in the description field.
Adding many language examples when one strong example would be clearer.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It helps authors create agent skills that are discoverable, concise, and tested before deployment.

faq.question: Can it create skills for Claude and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. It discusses personal skill locations for Claude Code and Codex and focuses on portable authoring patterns.

faq.question: Does it include executable code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes render-graphs.js, a Node.js helper that runs local Graphviz to generate diagrams.

faq.question: Is the executable helper required?
faq.answer: No. The main guidance works without it. The helper is only for rendering dot flowcharts.

faq.question: Does it test skills automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides test design methods and examples, but users must run and evaluate the scenarios.

faq.question: Why are pressure scenarios included?
faq.answer: They reveal whether agents follow a skill when speed, authority, or sunk cost creates pressure to skip it.
